articles.title: Baby Egrets With Punk Rock Hair Win Big Photo Prize!
articles.summary: Imagine three tiny birds with spiky feathers that look just like punk rock mohawks! That photo won the grand prize in Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photography contest. The photographer, Jacqueline Burke, is a 64-year-old vet who snapped the shot at a bird breeding ground in New Jersey. She says the chicks were full of attitude, squabbling nonstop. The contest got over 17,000 entries from 108 countries. Other winners include a woman praying with oil lamps in India, a Navajo girl's coming-of-age ceremony, and a rare chameleon. The photos are all about capturing amazing moments in nature and culture.

articles.title: How a Group of Women Weavers in Peru Are Saving Their Traditions and Making Money
articles.summary: High in the Andes mountains of Peru, Quechua women have been weaving beautiful textiles for centuries. But as the world changes, they needed a way to earn money without leaving their villages. That's where Awamaki comes in! This nonprofit helps nine cooperatives of 174 women weavers sell their goods and welcome tourists. The women raise alpacas, shear their wool, spin it into yarn, and weave it into amazing fabrics. With climate change making farming harder, having a steady income helps families buy food, medicine, and school supplies. Best of all, the women get to keep their traditions alive while gaining independence.

articles.title: Azerbaijan Destroys New Armenian Church in Artsakh, Experts Say
articles.summary: Satellite images show that Azerbaijan has destroyed a church in Stepanakert, the former capital of Artsakh (also called Nagorno-Karabakh). The Holy Mother of God Church was only built in 2019, after 12 years of construction. During the 2020 war, it was used as a bomb shelter. Now it's gone. Experts say this is part of a pattern of destroying Armenian cultural sites since 2023, when over 130,000 Armenians were forced to leave. The news comes just days before the 111th anniversary of the Armenian Genocide, when 1.5 million Armenians were killed by the Ottoman Empire. Turkey and Azerbaijan still deny that genocide happened.
